file_name: Dalal et al. - 2025 - OncoScan Web Based Deep Learning System for Multi Class Skin Lesion Diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper presents a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based system for multi-class skin lesion diagnosis. It utilized the ISIC 2020 dataset, performed image preprocessing (resizing, normalization, augmentation), and trained a CNN architecture to classify skin lesions into cancerous and non-cancerous categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for multi-class skin lesion diagnosis using the ISIC 2020 dataset. It details specific methodology (data acquisition, preprocessing, model architecture), reports original quantitative results (94.6% accuracy, class-wise metrics), and includes a confusion matrix and comparison with state-of-the-art methods. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE INSPECT), it contains original research results rather than being a review or survey.

title: OncoScan Web Based Deep Learning System for Multi Class Skin Lesion Diagnosis
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Signal Processing and Effective Communication Technologies (INSPECT)
brief_summary: This study presents a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based system for multi-class skin lesion diagnosis to address the subjectivity and errors of traditional dermatological methods. The model was trained on the ISIC 2020 dataset, which included 2,414 dermoscopic images categorized into seven cancerous and five non-cancerous lesion types. Data preprocessing involved resizing, normalization, and augmentation, with the dataset split into 80% training and 20% validation sets using stratified sampling. The proposed CNN architecture achieved a validation accuracy of 94.6%, with class-wise precision, recall, and F1-scores demonstrating competitive performance across all categories. The results indicate that the automated approach can effectively support dermatologists in early diagnosis, although future work will explore hybrid attention mechanisms and quantum-enhanced learning to further improve robustness.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion diagnosis (categorizing dermoscopic images into specific cancerous and non-cancerous lesion types)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'multi-class skin lesion classification' task. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for seven cancerous lesion types (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis) and mentions five non-cancerous types (though only cancerous classes were used in the final experiments). The output is a predicted label selected from the class with the highest likelihood (probability), evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset comprising cancerous and non-cancerous skin images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, used as the main dataset for the investigation. It contains dermoscopic pictures categorized into seven types of skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model, with 80% of the data (1,931 images) allocated to this set. Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming) and class weight adjustments were applied to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for performance assessment and hyperparameter tuning, with 20% of the data (483 images) allocated to this set. Stratified sampling was used to maintain class distribution. The validation set remained completely unseen during training.
use.other: Non-cancerous lesions (Skin Warts, Scabies, Infectious Erythema, Impetigo, Chickenpox) were mentioned as part of the broader ISIC 2020 categories but were explicitly not included in the current experiments.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To maintain class distribution in the training and validation sets.
To evaluate model performance by comparing predicted labels with ground truth labels and analyzing true/false positives/negatives.
To assess classifier effectiveness for each specific skin cancer class, balancing false positives and false negatives.
To assess the overall proportion of correct predictions.
To address class imbalance during training.
To enhance feature extraction and address class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing; it mentions that statistical significance testing, like confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, is planned for future work.

ISIC 2020.notes: The proposed model outperformed state-of-the-art methods listed in Table III. Confusion matrix analysis showed strong diagonal dominance, with high confidence for Nevus and Melanoma. Occasional misclassifications were observed between Pigmented Benign Keratosis and Seborrheic Keratosis.

architecture_details.layers: Convolutional layers, pooling layers, and fully connected layers
architecture_details.description: A custom CNN architecture with optimized convolutional and pooling layers. The network includes convolutional layers for feature extraction, pooling layers, and fully connected layers that take flattened output from previous layers to produce final classification probabilities.

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU on AWS SageMaker
training_details.loss_function: Class-weighted loss functions (to address class imbalance)
training_details.regularization: Dropout layers, data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming)

classification_or_output_head.description: Output layer producing probabilities for each possible class; predicted label selected from the class with the highest likelihood.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ResNet-50 with Channel and Spatial Attention Modules (Hybrid Attention-based Deep Learning framework)
Ensemble learning strategy (integrating multiple deep learning models)
Support Vector Machines (SVM) with Otsu thresholding
SVM-based system with ABCD rule-based features, GLCM, and HOG
Deep Neural Network with Bio-inspired Optimization
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a standard input dimension (specifically 224 × 224 pixels)
Image normalization (scaling pixel values using dataset mean and standard deviation)
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, and zooming) to address class imbalance
Grayscale conversion (using weighted sum of RGB components: 0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B)
Noise removal using a median filter (nonlinear filter to suppress salt-and-pepper noise while preserving edges)
Image segmentation using Mean Shift Clustering algorithm
Feature extraction using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture features (contrast and homogeneity)
Stratified sampling for dataset splitting to maintain class distribution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance feature extraction and address class imbalance. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied during training to enhance feature extraction and address class imbalance. Specific axis or probabilities were not reported.
Applied during training to enhance feature extraction and address class imbalance. Specific zoom ranges or factors were not reported.
Used to address class imbalance during training by adjusting the contribution of minority classes to the learning process.

conclusions: The proposed CNN-based system achieved a validation accuracy of 87% and a training accuracy of 93%, demonstrating high capability for extracting and learning discriminative features for multi-class skin lesion diagnosis.
The model outperformed state-of-the-art methods including ResNet50, MobileNetV2, and SVM+GLCM in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the ISIC 2020 dataset.
The system effectively identified common conditions such as Nevus and Melanoma with high confidence, as evidenced by class-wise metrics and confusion matrix analysis.
Limitations include occasional misclassifications between visually similar lesion types, specifically Pigmented Benign Keratosis and Seborrheic Keratosis.

The automated approach has the potential to support dermatologists in early diagnosis, increase reliability, and contribute to better patient outcomes by reducing subjectivity and human error associated with traditional visual examination.

Future work will focus on integrating advanced deep learning techniques such as hybrid attention mechanisms (combining ResNet-50 with Channel and Spatial Attention Modules), ensemble strategies, and quantum-enhanced learning to improve robustness and generalizability.

Future research will also include statistical significance testing (confidence intervals and hypothesis testing) and the integration of clinical metadata such as patient history and lesion localization to enhance clinical utility.
important_remarks: The study lacks an independent external test set; evaluation was performed solely on a held-out validation set (20% of ISIC 2020), limiting the assessment of generalizability to unseen populations.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., confidence intervals or hypothesis testing) was reported; the authors note this as future work, meaning the superiority over baselines is not statistically validated.
The model was trained exclusively on the ISIC 2020 dataset, which may introduce dataset-specific bias and limit external validity compared to multi-center or diverse clinical cohorts.

Five non-cancerous lesion types (Skin Warts, Scabies, Infectious Erythema, Impetigo, Chickenpox) mentioned in the abstract were explicitly excluded from the experiments, restricting the system's scope to only seven cancerous classes.

Performance issues were noted for visually similar classes, specifically Pigmented Benign Keratosis and Seborrheic Keratosis, indicating potential confusion between these specific diagnostic categories.

The paper is a conference proceeding (IEEE INSPECT 2025) with a relatively small sample size (2,414 images), which may be insufficient for robust deep learning generalization without extensive external validation.

Comparison with state-of-the-art methods (Table III) involves baselines trained on different datasets (ISIC 2018, HAM10000, ISIC 2019), making direct performance comparisons potentially confounded by dataset differences.
